preFix.title: Spinning a bouclé yarn
preFix.subtitle: Creating looped texture by over-plying a singles under tension
preFix.excerpt: Bouclé forms when a high-twist singles is plied around a smooth binder yarn while held under tension, causing the excess twist to buckle into loops. The technique uses three strands: a singles, a binder, and a locking thread. Careful control of the tensioned lazy kate is what separates consistent loops from an uneven tangle.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from published art yarn spinning guides and spinning guild teaching materials covering three-strand bouclé construction. The singles-plus-binder-plus-locker method is the standard documented approach. Tensioned lazy kate technique widely covered in handspinning reference books. No single public domain text.

content.text: Bouclé yarn is a structural yarn rather than a straightforward spun-and-plied construction. The looped texture forms because a
high-twist singles
is plied around a taut
binder yarn
: the singles has so much stored twist energy that when it meets the resistance of the taut binder, the excess twist has nowhere to go and buckles into loops. A
locker thread

is then plied back over the whole structure in the opposite direction to hold the loops in place. This three-strand construction is the method that produces consistent, even loops rather than the irregular clumped texture you get from simpler over-plied yarn.
Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair and roll up sleeves before sitting at the wheel.
Spinning the high-twist singles

The singles must be over-twisted by a significant margin relative to what a balanced yarn requires. Set your wheel to a high drive ratio (12:1 or higher) and treadle briskly while releasing fibre slowly. The resulting singles should kink and coil back on itself when held slack: this is correct and necessary. Spin with
Z-twist
. The fibre weight is less critical than the twist level; thicker singles will produce larger, more pronounced loops.
Wind the singles onto one bobbin. You will need approximately twice as much singles yardage as your planned finished yarn length, because the singles feeds faster than the binder during plying.
Choosing and preparing the binder and locker

The binder should be smooth, strong, and fine: a fine commercial cotton, silk thread, or tightly spun strong singles works well. The binder needs to hold firm under the tension of the looping singles without stretching. Wind the binder onto a separate bobbin or keep it on a cone.
The locker thread performs a different role from the binder: it is plied back over the finished singles-plus-binder structure in
S-twist
to hold the loops in place. Use a similar fine, smooth thread to the binder. Keep it wound on a separate bobbin.
Plying: the three-strand method
Place the singles bobbin on the
tensioned lazy kate

with the brake set to moderate resistance. Keep the binder yarn on a separate holder at floor level, pulling it up under slight tension. The key arrangement: the binder runs taut and straight to the wheel; the singles feeds from the tensioned kate and loops around the taut binder.

Set the wheel to S-twist at a moderate ratio (6:1 to 8:1). Hold both the binder and the singles together in your forward hand as you begin treadling. The binder stays taut; the singles should be allowed to feed more freely. As the S-twist ply twist builds, the Z-twist singles will begin to loop against the taut binder. You control loop size by adjusting how freely you allow the singles to feed: more singles per centimetre of binder produces larger loops; less produces smaller, tighter ones. Keep the tension on the binder firm and consistent throughout.

Once the singles-and-binder ply is complete, wind onto a bobbin. For the locking pass, set the wheel back to Z-twist. Hold the singles-and-binder structure together with the locker thread and ply all three together with Z-twist. This locks the loops into position. The locker does not need to be tight; it just needs enough twist to hold the bouclé structure stable.
Finishing the skein

Wind the finished bouclé onto the niddy-noddy carefully; the loops can catch on each other. Tie the skein loosely in four places with a smooth contrast thread. Soak in warm water for 15-20 minutes, press dry in a towel without squeezing, and hang to dry. Bouclé should not be dried under tension; the loops need to relax into their final shape.
Troubleshooting

items.problem: The loops are inconsistent: large in some places and absent in others.
items.solution: The tension on the lazy kate is inconsistent. Check that the brake mechanism is applying even resistance throughout the bobbin's rotation. Inconsistent tension causes the singles to feed in bursts rather than steadily, which produces uneven loop sizes.

items.problem: The binder keeps snapping under the tension of the singles looping around it.
items.solution: Use a stronger or thicker binder. Fine cotton or a commercial strong thread is more reliable than a handspun singles for the binder role. Alternatively, reduce the twist level in the high-twist singles so it exerts less force against the binder.

items.problem: The loops are falling off and the structure looks messy after finishing.
items.solution: The locking pass did not have enough twist to secure the loops. Unwind the skein carefully and run a second locking pass over the structure with more Z-twist. Alternatively, the locker thread was too smooth and slipped; try a slightly textured thread that grips better.
